opp.ais_why:
• Product scope includes Merlin and Merlin Intake alongside iContract, iSource, iSupplier and iSave. The SF Description references the displacement narrative: 'With this tender, our company is pursuing the goal of subjecting the system currently in use (SAP Ariba) to a comprehensive review. The focus is on the question of whether the existing system continues to optimally cover the required functions and process steps or whether there are providers on the market who can better meet our requirements with innovative solutions.' 'Innovative solutions' is AI-adjacent but not AI-specific.
• Formal Evaluation, $735K deal in chemicals vertical. The Description continues: 'leveraging the profitability impact by procurement is a key factor amongst an improved efficiency and productivity and increased usability!' Extensive Avoma meeting volume (large response payload signals many recorded sessions) but specific notes could not be parsed within the response-size limits. BD_Engagement is blank but last activity 15-Apr-2026 and Formal Evaluation stage suggest active evaluation.
• Medium confidence: 'innovative solutions' language and Merlin scope indicate AI openness but no explicit AI-led RFP demand surfaced in retrievable evidence. The SAP Ariba displacement may be cost-driven (profitability impact emphasised). Boundary defaulted DOWN to 6 ‚Äî would raise to 7 with explicit Avoma evidence of buyer-side AI demand.

metrics.narrative: No quantified business case or ROI has been articulated by Wacker across any call, Next Step, or Salesforce field. The 9 Mar 2026 feedback call discussed two commercial scenarios (full suite vs document-commerce-only) and buyer requested updated proposal with adjusted user counts (~280 power users, ~500 business users, maintained 15% discount), but this is a pricing structure request, not a quantified pain or savings target (source: 9 Mar call notes in Next_Step__c). No Cost-of-Inaction, cycle-time reduction, headcount savings, or spend-under-management metric has been confirmed. The deal appears driven by a platform modernization mandate (replacing legacy system, scope: CLM, Sourcing, Intake, Supplier per opp name) rather than a quantified business outcome. The move: demand a joint ROI workshop with the Economic Buyer to quantify the value case (cost avoidance, cycle time, compliance risk reduction) and anchor it to Wacker's business objectives before shortlist decision.

competition.narrative: Wacker is evaluating six vendors in the RFP: SAP Ariba (incumbent and primary threat), GEP Worldwide, Coupa, iValua, JAGGAER, and Zycus (source: Salesforce Competitors__c field). Ariba is the incumbent ('poor scores compared to Ariba' per 9 Mar Next Step) and benefits from status-quo bias: the 9 Mar feedback call revealed 'business hesitation to switch as many users preferred legacy system' and buyer stated 'at the beginning… we don't like our tool, we wanna switch, and now there is often this argument of do we really need to switch' (source: Next_Step__c 9 Mar). Ariba's incumbency advantage is strong despite mediocre POC scores ('team leads said all tools were scored low by the evaluators with no understandable reason' per 9 Mar Next Step). The 24 Apr Next Step states Wacker is evaluating 'Best of Breed and Platform vendors,' indicating Ariba (platform) vs Zycus (best-of-breed AI-powered S2P) is a key trade-off. No direct competitive intelligence on GEP, Coupa, iValua, or JAGGAER positioning. How we win: Lead with Merlin AI differentiation (negotiation agent, intake automation, contract intelligence) as the wedge against Ariba's legacy UX and lack of agentic AI, and anchor it to a specific Wacker use case (e.g., supplier negotiation cycle time, contract compliance risk). Make Merlin the Phase 2 centerpiece and position Zycus as the AI-native platform vs Ariba's bolt-on AI. Demand a competitive review with the Economic Buyer to surface Ariba's weaknesses (change-management cost, ERP lock-in, lack of supplier collaboration innovation) and contrast with Zycus's modern UX and AI-first roadmap.

identify_pain.narrative: Wacker's pain is operational and strategic but not quantified. The 9 Mar feedback call revealed internal resistance to change: 'business hesitation to switch as many users preferred legacy system, affecting recommendation survey scores' and buyer stated 'at the beginning… we don't like our tool, we wanna switch, and now there is often this argument of do we really need to switch from the business' (source: 9 Mar call notes in Next_Step__c). This indicates the original pain (dissatisfaction with legacy system) has cooled as the evaluation progressed, and status-quo bias is now a risk. The pain owner is unclear: procurement (Nina Schepetz, Sebastien Hu) drove the RFP, but business users (category, IT, legal across 26 stakeholders) are resisting. The 24 Apr Next Step states scope was 'reduced to SRM,' suggesting the pain is narrower than originally scoped (full S2P platform). No specific operational pain (cycle time, compliance risk, supplier onboarding delays, contract leakage) or financial pain (cost, savings target, ROI) has been articulated. The pain is inferred as 'legacy system modernization' but not confirmed as a burning platform. Gap: no named pain owner at exec level, no quantified impact, and cooling urgency. The move: re-anchor the pain with the Economic Buyer by surfacing a specific operational or financial consequence of NOT switching (e.g., regulatory compliance risk, supplier risk exposure, contract renewal leakage), and identify the C-level sponsor who owns that pain.

The move: once shortlisted, immediately request Wacker's standard MSA template, InfoSec questionnaire, and ERP/systems integration requirements, and align with EY on implementation scoping to avoid SOW bottlenecks during negotiation.

decision_process.narrative: Wacker is running a formal, committee-driven RFP process across multiple vendors (Zycus, SAP Ariba, GEP, Coupa, iValua, JAGGAER per Salesforce Competitors__c). The process sequence visible in Next Step: RFI response (pre-Nov 2025) → onsite demo at Burghausen (Nov 2025) → shortlisted 10 Nov 2025 → POC (Feb 2-11, 2026 across EMEA/APAC/US teams) → feedback session 9 Mar → RFP launched 8 May, due 2 Jun (source: Next_Step__c 21 May, 8 May) → RFP submitted 8 Jun → awaiting shortlist decision (originally planned end of March, now 3 months overdue per Next_Step__c 9 Apr). EY is involved as SI partner (source: Next_Step__c 21 May 'EY as partner'). The 9 Mar call revealed internal friction: evaluators scored all tools low 'with no understandable reason,' and team leads disagreed with user feedback, indicating a fragmented decision-making process (source: Next_Step__c 9 Mar). The approval chain is unknown: no visibility into who signs off at each gate (shortlist, contract, budget). The 26 Contact Roles span procurement (Nina Schepetz, Sebastien Hu), IT (Rudolf Strasser, Gerhard Hangoebl, Herbert Hangoebl), legal (Dr. Frauke Bemberg), digital process owner (Lilian Hansbauer), and category/sustainability (multiple), but no single Decision Maker or Executive Sponsor is named. Gap: no confirmed approval chain, no single point of accountability, and no visibility into who breaks ties when the committee is deadlocked. The move: map the formal approval process (who shortlists, who signs the contract, who approves the 735K budget) and identify the single executive who can drive consensus when the committee fragments.

decision_criteria.narrative: Wacker's evaluation criteria are partially visible from the POC process (Feb-Mar 2026) and RFP structure (Apr-Jun 2026). The 9 Mar feedback call revealed that Wacker scored all vendors (Zycus, Ariba, others) low, with 'critical feedback from users, poor scores compared to Ariba, however team leads said all tools were scored low by the evaluators with no understandable reason' (source: Next_Step__c 9 Mar). Specific usability gaps flagged during POC: approval-workflow notifications missing (approvers didn't receive bell-icon alerts), ASN fields (batch/best-before) absent, sourcing events not starting, iContract module complexity (source: 9 Mar call notes). Wacker also requested information on iManage and iRisk modules to decide scope (source: Next_Step__c 9 Apr 'Share information in iManage and iRisk/Risk Radar'). The 24 Apr Next Step states scope was 'reduced to SRM' and Wacker is evaluating 'Best of Breed and Platform vendors,' indicating a platform-vs-point-solution trade-off is a criterion. AI/Merlin positioning: Next Step 9 Apr lists 'Share Gen AI Use Cases' as a deliverable, suggesting AI capability is a criterion, but no confirmation Wacker prioritized it. Gap: no formal scoring rubric, weighted criteria, or decision-maker sign-off on what drives shortlist. The criteria are inferred from POC feedback and RFP scope, not confirmed by the buyer. The move: request the formal evaluation scorecard and decision rubric from the RFP process, and validate which criteria (usability, AI, platform scope, pricing) carry the most weight with the decision committee.

Buyer flagged 24 Apr: 'Wacker was not happy with the pricing.' Revised pricing provided 15 Apr at 735K EUR, but no confirmation of acceptance. 9 Mar call discussed two commercial scenarios (full suite vs document-commerce-only) and buyer requested updated proposal with adjusted user counts (~280 power, ~500 business users) and maintained 15% discount. Zycus committed to deliver updated pricing (source: 9 Mar call), but buyer went dark before follow-up. 9 Mar feedback call revealed 'business hesitation to switch as many users preferred legacy system, affecting recommendation survey scores' and buyer stated 'at the beginning… we don't like our tool, we wanna switch, and now there is often this argument of do we really need to switch from the business' (source: 9 Mar call notes + transcript). Shortlist decision planned end of March 2026 (source: Next_Step__c 9 Mar timeline), final decision by June 2026 (source: 9 Mar call). Both are now overdue (today 26 Jun). Close date 30 Sep (96 days) is not credible given 5+ weeks of silence and no EB/contract process started. Risk: deal is slipping or dead; close date will move to Q4 2026 or Q1 2027 if it re-engages.

Pricing revised 15 Apr but not validated. AI/Merlin positioning unclear. Scope reduced to SRM (24 Apr), suggesting Wacker is narrowing to core pain. Risk: usability concerns unresolved, no confirmation Zycus addressed POC gaps, and no Economic Buyer validation of fit.

hard.ais_why:
• Product scope includes Merlin and Merlin Intake alongside iContract, iSource, iSupplier and iSave. The SF Description references the displacement narrative: 'With this tender, our company is pursuing the goal of subjecting the system currently in use (SAP Ariba) to a comprehensive review. The focus is on the question of whether the existing system continues to optimally cover the required functions and process steps or whether there are providers on the market who can better meet our requirements with innovative solutions.' 'Innovative solutions' is AI-adjacent but not AI-specific.
• Formal Evaluation, $735K deal in chemicals vertical. The Description continues: 'leveraging the profitability impact by procurement is a key factor amongst an improved efficiency and productivity and increased usability!' Extensive Avoma meeting volume (large response payload signals many recorded sessions) but specific notes could not be parsed within the response-size limits. BD_Engagement is blank but last activity 15-Apr-2026 and Formal Evaluation stage suggest active evaluation.
• Medium confidence: 'innovative solutions' language and Merlin scope indicate AI openness but no explicit AI-led RFP demand surfaced in retrievable evidence. The SAP Ariba displacement may be cost-driven (profitability impact emphasised). Boundary defaulted DOWN to 6 ‚Äî would raise to 7 with explicit Avoma evidence of buyer-side AI demand.

Risk: status-quo bias may kill the project or favor Ariba by default despite mediocre scores.
